intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

body.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

draft.title: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments
draft.summary: Drafting your prototype from the swipes…
draft.html:
a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

Building your first draft…

data.intent: a personal dashboard for tracking long-term health experiments

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer a single high-level snapshot of health trends over raw data tables
facade.axisTargeted: aggregation level — granular daily data vs weekly rollups
facade.label: Weekly summary card
facade.acceptImplies: User wants time-compressed views; design around period-over-period patterns and aggregate metrics
facade.rejectImplies: User wants granular visibility into daily data; design should surface individual data points and daily variance

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer gentle, approachable visual containers over rigid geometric elements for a health tracking interface
facade.label: Soft rounded cards
facade.acceptImplies: Explore curved corners, organic shapes, and gentle visual hierarchy for data display; consider rounded buttons and flowing layouts
facade.rejectImplies: Test sharper geometric forms, crisp corners, and linear grid-based layouts; consider angular cards and structured blocky components

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer gentle, rounded data visualization over sharp angular charts—suggesting a warm, organic approach to health tracking feels less clinical
facade.label: Soft graph lines
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should use curved, flowing visual elements for data—rounded corners, smooth gradients, hand-drawn or organic typography. Mood should feel natural and human-centered rather than sterile or tech-forward.
facade.rejectImplies: Users prefer crisp, geometric, structured visuals—sharp lines, grids, sans-serif type, high contrast. The dashboard should feel precise and data-native rather than softened or approachable.

evidence.hypothesis: Users prefer a single high-level snapshot of health trends over raw data tables
evidence.implication: User wants time-compressed views; design around period-over-period patterns and aggregate metrics

facade.hypothesis: Users of health dashboards prefer persistent left navigation paired with card-based content over tab-based layouts, because it allows quick switching between experiments while keeping content scannable.
facade.label: Sidebar with cards
facade.acceptImplies: Explore more sidebar-anchored layouts; test card density and grouping; consider which experiments live where
facade.rejectImplies: Shift toward tabs, full-width lists, or top navigation; revisit whether persistent nav is actually necessary for this use case

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer conversational, low-pressure language over clinical health tracking terminology
facade.axisTargeted: Voice and Personality — friendly vs professional
facade.label: Casual check-ins
facade.acceptImplies: The dashboard should use warm, approachable language ('How are you feeling?' vs 'Enter vitals'). Tone leans conversational. Playful microcopy welcomed.
facade.rejectImplies: Users expect professional, clinical language ('Baseline metrics', 'Data entry', 'Health parameters'). Tone should be authoritative and structured. Friendly voice is a liability.

facade.hypothesis: Users prefer fluid, eased transitions over instant state changes when viewing health data
facade.axisTargeted: Motion and Behavior — animated transitions vs instant updates
facade.label: Smooth slide-in
facade.acceptImplies: Dashboard data refreshes with gentle animations; new metrics glide into place; past experiments fade out smoothly
facade.rejectImplies: User prefers instant, snappy updates with no animation; data appears immediately without transition effects

axes.label: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
axes.poleA: All at once or step by step?

axes.label: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers minimal clinical aesthetics vs. warm, human-centered visual design for health data
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Iris

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants data-dense dashboard layout vs. progressive disclosure with one metric at a time
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Prism

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user responds to motivational/encouraging tone vs. neutral, factual reporting voice
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Lumen

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants celebratory, gamified feedback vs. calm, meditative tracking environment
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Aura

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user prefers raw numbers and charts vs. narrative summaries and insights
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Facet

scout_assignments.probe_axis: Whether user wants instant feedback on actions vs. slow, contemplative animations and transitions
scout_assignments.reason: Cold start: first question for Echo

facade.hypothesis: Users find circular progress indicators more emotionally engaging and less clinical than linear bars when tracking health experiments over time
facade.axisTargeted: visualization form — circular vs linear, organic vs mechanical
facade.label: Progress rings instead of bars
facade.acceptImplies: Design the dashboard with circular/radial progress metrics (rings, dials, gauges); prioritize emotional resonance and approachability in data display; embrace rounded, organic visual language for all metrics
facade.rejectImplies: Stick with linear progress bars and traditional rectangular charts; prioritize clarity and efficiency over warmth; consider a more minimalist, data-dense interface aesthetic
